speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Linda Behr. The date is March 1, 1997. I'm interviewing Lyonell Fliss in Johannesburg, South Africa. Tape six. You went back to Bucharest--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Bucharest five years ago to a reunion of the university. It was after 23 years of leaving Romania. I found the same friendship that I left there with my former colleagues. It was very emotioned. I will repeat this trip this year when I will celebrate 40 years of our graduation. My wife Anita, former [INAUDIBLE]. I met some friends and we spent already nine years together. Nine happy years. Which took me out of the depression and the pain of the days of my first wife. And we tried to conduct a normal my life in South Africa. Under the difficult circumstances this country is going through. My main occupation is still civil engineering. And I try to excel in what I'm doing. And to my satisfaction, some of my works were recognized. I look back into all my trip in life, which spanned over six decades, and I think that I am qualified to draw some conclusions. From my childhood through the tragic experience that I had with the Germans in the first encounter, and thinking back what ordinary German soldier is capable to do to a child, I came to the conclusion that the German nation, as a whole, it's one of the biggest failures of this world. There may be individuals which are different, but morally, the German population, the German nation, it's a shame for humanity. In my opinion, they must be isolated at this moment, because I don't believe that anything changed in this period since the world-- the Second World War, and let either live in a quarantine, not to propagate this sort of inhumane morality. The fact that many people are misled by the fact that their country is clean and they are very precise doesn't mean that they are proper human beings. If a soldier like that one, which I called the brave German soldier, which was nothing special, can do what he did, the whole nation is infected, and need to be kept apart from the rest of humanity. There is no excuse for them. And when they talk about reconciliation with their victims, I do not believe one word. They misled us so many times, and they proved to be the biggest liars of the whole world. They are the ones who produced Goebbels. They produced Hitler. They produced all their SS and the Nazis. But more than this, they produced the German nation, which as a whole, is a shame for humanity. Not long time ago, I attended a reconciliation meeting organized by the Goethe Institute of Germany. I heard what was said there. I wasn't able to express my opinions, because the meeting was too short. It is a cultural organization, Goethe Institute. If they wanted to have reconciliation with the Jews who brought culture to Germany-- and they can't deny this, that their culture is mostly a Jewish product-- they shouldn't have a Goethe Institute, they should have a [non-English speech] Institute. And I'm not so sure, I'll check if they do have an Einstein Academy. Their Academy of Sciences, I do not believe that it's called the Einstein Academy. The greatest genius that the humanity produced until this time. If they still ignore this fact, it shows that reconciliation is not there. The German nation, if they wanted to have reconciliation, they should have a Yom Hashoah as there is in Israel, the same day. This is what I would have asked them as a minimum, and to educate their children into Judaic studies, to make them understand what Judaism, the values of the Judaism, the humanity that was spread over the whole civilized world is, and what they did by burning the Torahs, by burning the people who produced all this. So this is the reason that I'm very pessimistic about the capacity of the Germans to be able to reconcile. And we shouldn't forget ever what they did, and how they misled us so many times. My message to the rest of the Jewish people is not to forget what happened to us in those times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Lyonell. Lyonell, who are these in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are the grandparents from my mother's side, the Blitzstein couple. Jacques Blitzstein and Sophia Blitzstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in the last decade of the last century, 1890-something. It was taken in Galatzi. As you can see, very smart, very confident, and very good-looking. Here it's shown my grandmother, Sophia Blitzstein, with three of her children. On the right hand side is my mother, Adela, with two of her sisters. In this photograph, you can see on the extreme left my grandmother, Sophia Blitzstein, and on the right, her mother, my great-grandmother. In the center, between the two of them, is my mother, Adela, and around, the other children of my grandmother. Here it's in the center shown my mother, a year before her marriage to my father. On the right hand side, the two boys are her brothers. The extreme right is Harry, and the one next to him, Philip. And there are three cousins. Max, on the left hand side, he was a fighter in the Haganah and was killed in action. The wedding photograph of my parents, [? Lupo ?] and Adela, in the house of my mother's parents in Galatzi, 1934. A picture taken during the happy days of a holiday at one of the Romanian spa resorts, Dorna, in Bucovina. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The two families, Fliss and Blitzstein, went together with all their children, just before my mother got married to my father. They were still engaged. My mother is on the second row, in the center. My grandfather, Jacques Blitzstein, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] short before his death, which occurred a year after my birth, in 1936. Myself in the pram, equipped for the first winter of my life, in front of my father's shop in Strada Bratianu, Iasi, with my parents. On the laps of my mother on the balcony of our first house, Strada Vasile Alecsandri, Iasi, age one. From Strada Vasile Alecsandri, we moved into a new flat in Strada Bratianu, one of the central streets of Iasi, where the photograph was taken from the balcony on a sunny day, 1939, without even guessing what will happen in the same place in two years' time. Next to me, the baby, Bertina Grossman, with her mother. She was meant to be my future wife. In 1939 I was four years old, and following the good tradition of stuffing with lots of food, my mother took me to the Gura Humorului spa, famous for its good food, mineral waters, fresh air, hotels. My parents always wanted me to be very smart, smartly dressed, so they bought me a nice caracal hat for the winter of 1941. Unfortunately, because of the anti-Semitic rules, I had to wear a yellow star. And walking with the yellow star, and with this hat, some older children caught me, beat me up, and stole my hat. I was very cross. This was in 1941, when there were rumors that the Jews may be deported to Transnistria. And for this reason, my parents shaved my head to prepare me for going to a concentration camp. In 1942, when the Germans were very busy implementing their final solution to the Jewish problem, in Romania started preparations for the same thing. So the first step was a census of the Jewish population, to know the numbers and the names with whom the local Nazis and the government shall deal with. So a form needed to be filled in with the name and the address of every person having Jewish blood. It wasn't a matter of Jewish religion, it was Jewish blood-- indifferent of the proportion of blood, Jewish blood, to other kind of blood. It was a pure racialistic act, and my parents hesitated a long time before they submitted this statement, because of course they were scared that based on this, I'll be also collected and sent to extermination camps. The statement, it's signed by my father. I was a minor. I was seven. And fortunately, the extermination or the deportation of the Jewish population didn't take place. This picture was taken '42 or '43, when I was a pupil in the primary school in the synagogue in Iasi. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] In spite of the harsh conditions, we tried to keep clean and smart and disciplined. Immediately after the war, after moving to Bucharest, in the summer, in the hot summer of Romania, everyone was spending couple of cool hours at the beach. There were big lakes around Bucharest with nice warm water and sand. And here you can see myself in the center, my father on the left. On the right, my father's brother, my uncle David, who is a survivor of the death train of 1941, Black Sunday. He himself survived because he was a very strong young man. Very good looking, tall, Johnny Weissmuller type. A photograph from the training camp of the Romanian army. As an officer in training in 1953, myself on the right hand side, at Deva in Transylvania. In front of the magistrate court in Bucharest, with my first wife, Lillianna, at our civil wedding. Behind us, between us, in the second row, her father Franz. On the left side, that's my mother, and behind her, my father. And left of my mother on the second row, that's Lillianna's mother, [? Annie. ?] In Israel, at the border to Lebanon, at the Russian [? Ikra, ?] a popular touristic place, my mother. Next to her, my uncle David Fliss, my father's brother, who now lives in Argentina. And next to him, my father. A friend next to them. 1972. Myself as a soldier in the Israeli army in 1971, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when I served until leaving to South Africa in 1975. If I'm a survivor of the Holocaust and of the other events which followed, this plate, it's a survivor and a half. It survived the looting of [? Anike ?] in the pogrom there. It survived all the damage done by the German heroes in our flat. It survived the Russian bombing of Iasi. It survived the American and British bombing of Bucharest, and it arrived intact into the little luggage that my parents took with them when they immigrated to Israel. And from there, in my suitcase to South Africa. It is the last remnant of the great chinaware empire of my grandfather, Jacques Blitzstein, who gave as a present the whole set to my parents, on the occasion of their wedding. I would like now, in the end, to introduce my wife, Anita, who through her experience with me for a number of years, and her experience with the Holocaust issue through the Zionist Federation, may pass to you her message.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Anita. I was born Cohen, and I was born in Capetown, South Africa. And therefore being a very protected western European, I really feel that going through the travels of Lyonell makes me feel very inadequate, compared to the protected life that I have led. And it has really shown me what some people have really gone through and have really survived. And I think it's a wonderful gesture that this foundation has given us the opportunity to express our feelings and sentiments and gone through a nostalgic ride. It is a wish that I wanted to do, myself, tribute to Lyonell, to perhaps have written his autobiography, perhaps when we were retired. And you have given us this particular opportunity, and saved me all the time and the stress, and to put it into words and to reality and to bring it into a very special meaning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to conclude by giving my thanks to the foundation for enabling me, for the first time, to put my story together, and to keep this as a document of what human people can do to each other. But is difficult for me to call human those who created the Holocaust. And my final thought will be an optimistic one, is that we still will survive in a moral society. I think that all of us, we have to be very, very careful when we judge people who went through all the Holocaust on one or the other side, and to make sure it will never do an injustice to those who saved lives of innocent people in these terrible times.
